title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

infobox.value: Футбольний клуб «Шахтар» Донецьк Football Club Shakhtar Donetsk

[Arena Lviv](/wiki/Arena_Lviv "Arena Lviv"), [Lviv Oblast](/wiki/Lviv_Oblast "Lviv Oblast")  [Henryk Reyman Municipal Stadium](/wiki/Henryk_Reyman_Municipal_Stadium "Henryk Reyman Municipal Stadium"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") (European cups)

([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Футбольний клуб Шахтар Донецьк) is a Ukrainian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club that was based in the city of [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk") until 2014 when, due to the [War in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)"), the club was forced to move to [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv"), and played matches in Lviv (2014–2016) and in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") (2017–2020) whilst having its office headquarters and training facilities in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv").[[1]](#cite_note-ShaktarKharkiv-1) In May 2020, Shakhtar started to play home matches at [NSC Olimpiyskyi](/wiki/Olimpiyskiy_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex") in Kyiv.[[2]](#cite_note-Olimpiyskiy-2) Since the beginning of the 2023–24 season, Shakhtar has played home matches once again at [Arena Lviv](/wiki/Arena_Lviv "Arena Lviv").

**Football Club Shakhtar Donetsk** ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Футбольний клуб Шахтар Донецьк) is a Ukrainian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club that was based in the city of [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk") until 2014 when, due to the [War in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)"), the club was forced to move to [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv"), and played matches in Lviv (2014–2016) and in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") (2017–2020) whilst having its office headquarters and training facilities in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv").[[1]](#cite_note-ShaktarKharkiv-1) In May 2020, Shakhtar started to play home matches at [NSC Olimpiyskyi](/wiki/Olimpiyskiy_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex") in Kyiv.[[2]](#cite_note-Olimpiyskiy-2) Since the beginning of the 2023–24 season, Shakhtar has played home matches once again at [Arena Lviv](/wiki/Arena_Lviv "Arena Lviv").

Shakhtar has appeared in several European competitions and is often a participant in the UEFA Champions League. The club became the first club in independent Ukraine to win the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") in [2009](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_UEFA_Cup "2008–09 UEFA Cup"), the last year before the competition was revamped as the Europa League. FC Shakhtar Donetsk is one of two Ukrainian clubs, the other being [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv"), who have won a major UEFA competition.

The club formerly played its home matches in Donetsk at the newly built [Donbas Arena](/wiki/Donbass_Arena "Donbass Arena"), however due to the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") in 2014, the team were forced to relocate 1,000 kilometres (620 mi) to the west in Arena Lviv in the interim.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Following the winter break of the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_season "2016–17 FC Shakhtar Donetsk season") the club then moved again to the [Metalist Stadium](/wiki/Metalist_Oblast_Sports_Complex "Metalist Oblast Sports Complex") in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") (250 kilometres (160 mi) to the northwest of Donetsk) early in 2017.[[1]](#cite_note-ShaktarKharkiv-1) In May–July 2020 Shakhtar played home matches at [NSC Olimpiyskyi](/wiki/Olimpiyskiy_National_Sports_Complex "Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex") in Kyiv.[[2]](#cite_note-Olimpiyskiy-2)

Shakhtar Donetsk is one of Ukraine's most popular football clubs,[[4]](#cite_note-ShaktarIU27813-4) and is particularly favoured in the eastern [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas") region.[[4]](#cite_note-ShaktarIU27813-4) On 28 October 2021, Shakhtar had 1.4 million followers on Facebook, the highest in Ukraine.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The club draws its history from the very start of the Soviet football league competitions and is one of the oldest clubs in Ukraine. The club was a member of the Soviet [Voluntary Sports Society](/wiki/Voluntary_Sports_Societies_of_the_Soviet_Union "Voluntary Sports Societies of the Soviet Union") of Shakhtyor, having connections with other Soviet teams from [Karaganda](/wiki/FC_Shakhter_Karagandy "FC Shakhter Karagandy") ([Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan")), [Soligorsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtyor_Soligorsk "FC Shakhtyor Soligorsk") ([Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus")), among others. In the late Soviet period, Shakhtar was considered a tough mid-table club of the [Soviet Top League](/wiki/Soviet_Top_League "Soviet Top League") and a cup competition specialist after winning the [Soviet Cup](/wiki/Soviet_Cup "Soviet Cup") two years in a row in 1961 and 1962.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### The club's names and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The club's names and etymology")]

The team has played under the following names: Stakhanovets (1936–46), Shakhtyor (Shakhtar) (1946–92), and FC Shakhtar (1992–present).

The club has a meaningful association with the [Donets Basin](/wiki/Donets_Basin "Donets Basin") [underground coal-mining](/wiki/Coal_mining#Underground_mining "Coal mining") using [vertical mining shafts](/wiki/Shaft_sinking "Shaft sinking"), called *[Schacht](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schacht_(Bergbau) "de:Schacht (Bergbau)")* in German. This was taken over into Cyrillic *Шахтн*, end re-transcribed into Latin letters as, for example, *shakt* in English use. As part of the so-called [Stalin industrialisation](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin#1927–1931:_Dekulakisation,_collectivisation,_and_industrialisation "Joseph Stalin") and [Stakhanovite movement](/wiki/Stakhanovite_movement "Stakhanovite movement"), in 1936 the local football teams of Dynamo sports societies of Horlivka and Stalino (today Donetsk) established a joint team that represented the mining volunteer sports society Stakhanovets (later Shakhter). The team was transferred from the sports society for the [NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD") to a trade union "volunteered sports society" (DSO).

Following the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the DSO Stakhanovets changed its name to DSO Shakhter which in the Ukrainian SSR had its local corresponding nomenclature as DSS Shakhtar. The word "Shakhter" or "Shakhtar" means a coal miner working at a [sub-surface mine](/wiki/Underground_hard-rock_mining "Underground hard-rock mining"), shafted mine, shakhta is a derivative of shaft.

During the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") (1989–1992), the Donetsk club was reorganized and commercialized as a professional football team. It also made its Ukrainian name as its only name, Шахтар Донецьк, with *Shakhtar Donetsk* in English, *Schachtar Donezk* in German and *Szachtar Donieck* in Polish, the latter two being languages of countries that hosted international "home" games for the club due to the situation since 2014.

### Early years – first two decades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years – first two decades")]

See also: [FC Shakhtar Horlivka](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Horlivka "FC Shakhtar Horlivka") and [FC Metalurh Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Metalurh_Donetsk "FC Metalurh Donetsk")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/1937._Staxanovets.jpg/250px-1937._Staxanovets.jpg)](/wiki/File:1937._Staxanovets.jpg)

The team in 1937.

The Shakhtar club was originally formed on a decision of the [All-Union Council on Physical Culture and Sports](/wiki/All-Union_Council_on_Physical_Culture_and_Sports "All-Union Council on Physical Culture and Sports") of 3 April 1936. It was initially named **Stakhanovets**, meaning "the participant of [Stakhanovite movement](/wiki/Stakhanovite_movement "Stakhanovite movement")", which derived from [Aleksei Stakhanov](/wiki/Alexei_Stakhanov "Alexei Stakhanov"), a coal-miner in the [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas") and propaganda celebrity in 1935. The first team was based upon two other local teams, the participants of the All-Ukrainian [Spartakiads](/wiki/Spartakiad "Spartakiad"): *[Dynamo Horlivka](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Horlivka "FC Dynamo Horlivka")* and *Dynamo Stalino*. The first game was against [Dynamo Odesa](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Odesa "FC Dynamo Odesa") as part of the [1936 Cup of the Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/1936_Cup_of_the_Ukrainian_SSR "1936 Cup of the Ukrainian SSR") (at that time known as Ukrainian spring challenge) and took place on 12 May 1936 at Balitsky Stadium in [Horlivka](/wiki/Horlivka "Horlivka") (the first home stadium). The team that played as Stakhonovets Horlivka lost 3–2 after scoring the first goal by Mykhailo Pashchenko,[[a]](#cite_note-7) the second goal belonged to Boris Terentiev.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8)

Its first league game in [Group V](/wiki/1936_Soviet_Second_League "1936 Soviet Second League") took place on 24 May 1936 against *Dynamo Kazan* was even more disappointing, which miners lost 4–1. Stakhonovets that had on its roster 15 players left for [Kazan](/wiki/Kazan "Kazan") by train on 20 May.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) Beside players, as part of delegation there were representative of regional council of physical culture Gololobov and republican referee I.Rozanov.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) The team returned to Stalino on 28 May and the same day Gololobov in interview to newspaper "Stalinskiy rabochiy" told that "... the game in Kazan with local "Dynamo" was witnessed by 3,000 spectators.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) With the first minutes, the field hosts offered a high pace.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8)

The "Miners", who were road weary, could not respond with the same. On the 13th minute they conceded the first goal and by the end of first half, two more.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) In many respects the reason was poor performance of right halfback Kutsev (who played instead of K.Pashchenko) and right outside forward Korotynsky.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) Through their flank Dynamo players successfully attacked.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) In the first half Stakhanovets forwards looked bleak and uncertain.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) In the second half the game equalized and on 55th minute Fedor Manov opened score to Donetsk team goals.[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8) Final score is 4–1 in favor of the hosts."[[7]](#cite_note-urainform-8)

Nonetheless, the selective job conducted constructively by the club's administration allowed the club to compete successfully at the top level by the end of the 1930s. During the *war championship* of 1941, which was interrupted unexpectedly, the club defeated Soviet champions [Dynamo Moscow](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Moscow "FC Dynamo Moscow") and after about ten games were placed in fifth in the league. In the last game of that championship, played on 24 June, two days after the start of the [Great Patriotic War](/wiki/Great_Patriotic_War "Great Patriotic War"),[[8]](#cite_note-9) which they lost at home to [Traktor Stalingrad](/wiki/FC_Rotor_Volgograd "FC Rotor Volgograd").[[9]](#cite_note-10) During the war many players went to frontlines and perished among which are Ivan Ustinov, Ivan Putyatov, Volodymyr Shkurov, Ivan Horobets, Mykhailo Vasin and others.[[6]](#cite_note-footballnews-6) From the pre-war squad in 1945 there were left only three players Georgiy Bikezin, Mykola Kuznetsov, and Petro Yurchenko.[[6]](#cite_note-footballnews-6)

The All-Union coal mining society of Stakhanovite (Stakhanovets) had changed its name in July 1946 to **Shakhtyor** (Shakhter) and so did the Sports Society of Donbas Miners. The term Shakhtar or Shakhter (Russian variation) is occupational referring to a miner working in a subterranean mining shaft (see [shaft mining](/wiki/Shaft_mining "Shaft mining")), the word "shakhtar" is a local adaptation and derivative of shaft. In the Soviet Union, due to an elevated risk working underground, a subterranean mining job was more compensated in relation to other unskilled work and always in demand.

In 1950, [Viktor Fomin](/wiki/Viktor_Fomin "Viktor Fomin") was named [Ukrainian Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Ukrainian_Footballer_of_the_Year "Ukrainian Footballer of the Year"), despite the club finishing only 11th in the league. The first success for the team was in 1951, when it achieved third place in the [USSR Championship](/wiki/Soviet_Top_League "Soviet Top League"). The most notable player of that achievement was the [striker](/wiki/Forward_(association_football)#Striker "Forward (association football)") [Aleksandr Ponomarev](/wiki/Aleksandr_Ponomarev_(footballer,_born_1918) "Aleksandr Ponomarev (footballer, born 1918)"), who came to finish his football career in [Donbas](/wiki/Donbas "Donbas"), the region he was born in, and was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year for 1951. Despite the latest achievement, Shakhtar was relegated at the end of the 1952 season and as part of the re-organization of the team, former player [Aleksandr Ponomarev](/wiki/Aleksandr_Ponomarev_(footballer,_born_1918) "Aleksandr Ponomarev (footballer, born 1918)") became the head coach of the club. In 1954, Shakhtar under Ponomarev won the [Class B League](/wiki/Soviet_First_League "Soviet First League"), thus sealing a return to the top league.

### Cup triumphs and establishment in the Soviet League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cup triumphs and establishment in the Soviet League")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Oleg_Oshenkov_star.jpg/120px-Oleg_Oshenkov_star.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oleg_Oshenkov_star.jpg)

A star in the Shakhtar Walk of Fame in honor of [Oleg Oshenkov](/wiki/Oleg_Oshenkov "Oleg Oshenkov"), who as manager twice led Shakhtar to [Soviet Cup](/wiki/Soviet_Cup "Soviet Cup") victory.

In 1958, the players of the club received fewer yellow and red cards than any other team in the championship, for what the *[Sovetsky Sport](/wiki/Sovetsky_Sport "Sovetsky Sport")* newspaper awarded the club with the "Fair Play Award."[[10]](#cite_note-11)
In the 1960s, Shakhtar, under [Oleg Oshenkov](/wiki/Oleg_Oshenkov "Oleg Oshenkov")'s coaching, were three-time [USSR Cup](/wiki/USSR_Cup "USSR Cup") finalists, winning it twice in 1961 and 1962. Among the players playing for the club then where defenders [Viacheslav Aliabiev](/wiki/Viacheslav_Aliabiev "Viacheslav Aliabiev") and [Vladimir Salkov](/wiki/Vladimir_Salkov "Vladimir Salkov"). The club was nicknamed "The Cup Team" due to Shakhtar's success in vying for the trophy every year. The Miners' more notable achievements, however, occurred later from the mid-1970s to the early 1980s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/%D0%97%D1%96%D1%80%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%92%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%83%D1%85%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%90%D0%BB%D0%B5%D1%97_%D0%A1%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B8_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8F.jpeg/120px-%D0%97%D1%96%D1%80%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%92%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%83%D1%85%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%90%D0%BB%D0%B5%D1%97_%D0%A1%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B8_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8F.jpeg)](/wiki/File:%D0%97%D1%96%D1%80%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%92%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%8F_%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%83%D1%85%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D0%90%D0%BB%D0%B5%D1%97_%D0%A1%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%B8_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%8F.jpeg)

A star in the Shakhtar Walk of Fame in honor of [Vitaliy Starukhin](/wiki/Vitaliy_Starukhin "Vitaliy Starukhin"), considered by many fans the greatest player in the history of Shakhtar.[[11]](#cite_note-sport.segodnya.ua-12)

Despite the departure of the team's leader midfielder [Anatoliy Konkov](/wiki/Anatoliy_Konkov "Anatoliy Konkov"), in 1975, Shakhtar under management of former player [Vladimir Salkov](/wiki/Vladimir_Salkov "Vladimir Salkov"), earned second place in the [USSR Championship](/wiki/Soviet_Top_League "Soviet Top League") and received the right to represent the Soviet Union in [European competition](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"). At the end of the season, Shakhtar received the [Progress Cup](/wiki/Progress_Cup "Progress Cup") for making the biggest progress from previous season in the league. They received the award again in 1977. In 1978, Shakhtar finished third in the USSR Championship.[[12]](#cite_note-Виктор_Звягинцев:_Коньков_мне_объяс-13)

In 1979, the team finished second in the league campaign and its captain—[striker](/wiki/Forward_(association_football)#Striker "Forward (association football)") [Vitaliy Starukhin](/wiki/Vitaliy_Starukhin "Vitaliy Starukhin")—became the top scorer in the [USSR Championship](/wiki/Soviet_Top_League "Soviet Top League") with 26 goals scored, also being named [Soviet Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Soviet_Footballer_of_the_Year "Soviet Footballer of the Year"). The club was only two points away from the first place, despite having important players leaving the club before the season, and other important players receiving injuries.[[12]](#cite_note-Виктор_Звягинцев:_Коньков_мне_объяс-13)

Other important players besides Starukhin at the time were [Mykhaylo Sokolovskyi](/wiki/Mykhaylo_Sokolovskyi "Mykhaylo Sokolovskyi"), who went on to set a caps record for the club (for what he received the Club Loyalty Award in 1987), defenders [Viktor Zvyahintsev](/wiki/Viktor_Zvyahintsev "Viktor Zvyahintsev") and [Valeriy Horbunov](/wiki/Valeriy_Horbunov "Valeriy Horbunov"), who both made it numerous times to the 33 Top Players of the Soviet Championship lists, and goalkeeper [Yuriy Dehteryov](/wiki/Yuriy_Dehteryov "Yuriy Dehteryov"), who was named Soviet goalkeeper of the year and took third place for [Soviet Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Soviet_Footballer_of_the_Year "Soviet Footballer of the Year") in 1977.[[11]](#cite_note-sport.segodnya.ua-12)

Shakhtar twice, in 1980 and 1983, brought home the crystal USSR Cup to Donetsk and in 1983, it won the [USSR Super Cup](/wiki/Soviet_Super_Cup "Soviet Super Cup") over then-domestic league champions [Dnipro Dnipropetrovsk](/wiki/FC_Dnipro "FC Dnipro"). Shakhtar reached the [1983–84 European Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1983–84 European Cup Winners' Cup") quarter-final, and strikers [Viktor Hrachov](/wiki/Viktor_Hrachov "Viktor Hrachov") and [Serhiy N. Morozov](/wiki/Serhiy_Morozov_(footballer,_born_1961) "Serhiy Morozov (footballer, born 1961)") became joint top scorers of the tournament. In 1987, Shakhtar received the fewest yellow and red cards in the championship, for which the club was awarded the "Soviet Top League Fair Play Award" by *Man and Law* magazine.[[13]](#cite_note-14) Between 1982 and 1988, Shakhtar received the "Together With The Club" award five times, an award given for good organization of home games and behaviour of the home fans.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

During the [Perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika") period, the Shakhtar team of masters separated from the Shakhtar Donetsk sports society into a professional club. It was part of the so-called [khozraschet](/wiki/Khozraschet "Khozraschet") period or a "self-management", which was part of many Soviet organizations at that time. Other departments of the Shakhtar Donetsk sports society formed their own clubs independent from their parent organization.

### First decade in independent Ukraine – the beginning of the Akhmetov era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: First decade in independent Ukraine – the beginning of the Akhmetov era")]

In the newly independent Ukraine, Shakhtar, along with Dynamo Kyiv, became perennial first place competitors. A bombing-assassination took place at the team's stadium, killing club president [Akhat Bragin](/wiki/Akhat_Bragin "Akhat Bragin") in October 1995.[[15]](#cite_note-16) In 1996, [Rinat Akhmetov](/wiki/Rinat_Akhmetov "Rinat Akhmetov") took over as president and invested heavily in the club.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Despite Shakhtar not being a strong contender for the championship at the time, finishing second many times with a large point gap from the first-place position, they won the [Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Cup "Ukrainian Cup") three times, in [1995](/wiki/1995_Ukrainian_Cup_final "1995 Ukrainian Cup final") (under the management of former player [Vladimir Salkov](/wiki/Vladimir_Salkov "Vladimir Salkov")), [1997](/wiki/1997_Ukrainian_Cup_final "1997 Ukrainian Cup final") and [2001](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Ukrainian_Cup "2000–01 Ukrainian Cup"). In the [1997–98 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1997–98 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), Shakhtar were eliminated after a 5–2 aggregate loss to [Vicenza](/wiki/LR_Vicenza "LR Vicenza"), losing the first and second legs. Important players at the time were defenders [Serhiy Popov](/wiki/Serhiy_Popov "Serhiy Popov") and [Mykhaylo Starostyak](/wiki/Mykhaylo_Starostyak "Mykhaylo Starostyak"), goalkeeper [Dmytro Shutkov](/wiki/Dmytro_Shutkov "Dmytro Shutkov"), striker [Oleh Matvyeyev](/wiki/Oleh_Matvyeyev "Oleh Matvyeyev") (who was top scorer of the Premier League in the 1996–97 season), and [midfielders](/wiki/Midfielder "Midfielder") [Hennadiy Orbu](/wiki/Hennadiy_Orbu "Hennadiy Orbu"), [Valeriy Kryventsov](/wiki/Valeriy_Kryventsov "Valeriy Kryventsov") and [Ihor Petrov](/wiki/Ihor_Petrov "Ihor Petrov"). Most of the players playing for the team of the time came through the team's youth ranks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8F_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%86%D0%BA._%D0%A3%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B1%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%8C._%D0%A4%D0%9A_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80_033.jpg/250px-%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8F_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%86%D0%BA._%D0%A3%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B1%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%8C._%D0%A4%D0%9A_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80_033.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D1%8F_%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._%D0%94%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%86%D0%BA._%D0%A3%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%8C_%D0%B8_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B1%D0%B8%D1%82%D1%8C._%D0%A4%D0%9A_%D0%A8%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80_033.jpg)

Shakhtar's jersey with DCC instead of SCM

Towards the end of the decade, the team finally started to look like a team able to become champion. In 1999, a Shakhtar football academy was opened and now hosts football training for roughly 3,000 youth. In 2000, [Andriy Vorobey](/wiki/Andriy_Vorobey "Andriy Vorobey") was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year by *Komanda*, the first Shakhtar player in independent Ukraine to do so, and became the top scorer in the [2000–01 Vyshcha Liha](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Vyshcha_Liha "2000–01 Vyshcha Liha"). That year, Shakhtar competed in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") for the first time, drawn in a group with Lazio, Arsenal and Sparta Prague. They finished third in the group, qualifying for the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") after a 3–0 home win against Arsenal.

### First league triumph

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: First league triumph")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._fat17.tif/lossy-page1-250px-%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._fat17.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A4%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BA%D0%BE._fat17.tif)

President of the club, [Rinat Akhmetov](/wiki/Rinat_Akhmetov "Rinat Akhmetov"), shaking hands with captain [Anatoliy Tymoshchuk](/wiki/Anatoliy_Tymoshchuk "Anatoliy Tymoshchuk"), 2002 [Ukrainian Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Ukrainian_Footballer_of_the_Year "Ukrainian Footballer of the Year").

The club won its first Ukrainian Premier League title in the [2001–02 season](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Vyshcha_Liha "2001–02 Vyshcha Liha") under coach [Nevio Scala](/wiki/Nevio_Scala "Nevio Scala"), winning by a single point over Dynamo Kyiv. They were also victorious in the [2001–02 Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Ukrainian_Cup "2001–02 Ukrainian Cup"), defeating Dynamo 3–2 after extra time in the [final](/wiki/2002_Ukrainian_Cup_final "2002 Ukrainian Cup final").[[17]](#cite_note-18) Among the key players at the club at the time were captain [defensive midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder#Defensive_midfielder "Midfielder") [Anatoliy Tymoshchuk](/wiki/Anatoliy_Tymoshchuk "Anatoliy Tymoshchuk"), striker Andriy Vorobey, midfielder [Hennadiy Zubov](/wiki/Hennadiy_Zubov "Hennadiy Zubov") and defender [Mykhaylo Starostyak](/wiki/Mykhaylo_Starostyak "Mykhaylo Starostyak"). At the end of the season, Tymoshchuk, who emerged as the club's leader on the field, was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year by *Komanda* and *Ukrainskiy Football*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Mircea_Lucescu_Shakhtar_Donetsk-Arsenal_Kiev_2013_%281%29.jpg/250px-Mircea_Lucescu_Shakhtar_Donetsk-Arsenal_Kiev_2013_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mircea_Lucescu_Shakhtar_Donetsk-Arsenal_Kiev_2013_(1).jpg)

Manager [Mircea Lucescu](/wiki/Mircea_Lucescu "Mircea Lucescu") took over Shakhtar in 2004 and has led them to becoming the dominant force in the league.

After multiple managerial changes, in 2004 the Romanian [Mircea Lucescu](/wiki/Mircea_Lucescu "Mircea Lucescu") was invited to build a team in Shakhtar. After ten days at the club, he won the [2003–04 Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Ukrainian_Cup "2003–04 Ukrainian Cup") and after three months, for the first time in club history, the club made it to the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") group stage, which won him the 2004 [Romania Coach of the Year](/wiki/Romania_Coach_of_the_Year "Romania Coach of the Year") title.[[11]](#cite_note-sport.segodnya.ua-12) The strategy chosen was looking for young talented players in Brazil, which was to form the base of the attack, while the defence would supplied by largely Ukrainian talent in order to adjust to rules forcing teams to have a certain number of local players on the field.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

The large amount of Brazilians arriving at the club earned Shakhtar the nickname "the most Brazilian club in Europe".[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) They won their second Premier League title in the [2004–05 season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Vyshcha_Liha "2004–05 Vyshcha Liha"), but lost to Dynamo Kyiv in the inaugural [Ukrainian Super Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Super_Cup "Ukrainian Super Cup") tournament in 2004. They finished as runners up in the [2004–05 Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Ukrainian_Cup "2004–05 Ukrainian Cup"), losing to Dynamo in a penalty shoot-out the [final](/wiki/2005_Ukrainian_Cup_final "2005 Ukrainian Cup final").[[26]](#cite_note-27)

They retained the Premier League crown in the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Vyshcha_Liha "2005–06 Vyshcha Liha") and managed to avenge the defeat to Dynamo in the previous Super Cup by defeating them on penalties to win their first-ever Super Cup title.[[27]](#cite_note-28) At the end of the season, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year for by *Ukrainian Football* for the second time, becoming the first Shakhtar player to be named so more than once. Brazilian striker [Brandão](/wiki/Brand%C3%A3o_(footballer,_born_1980) "Brandão (footballer, born 1980)") became the league's joint top scorer.

Shakhtar appeared in all three editions of the [Channel One Cup](/wiki/Channel_One_Cup_(football) "Channel One Cup (football)"), winning the 2007 edition and finishing runners-up in 2008. Having missed out on the league title in [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Vyshcha_Liha "2006–07 Vyshcha Liha"),[[28]](#cite_note-29) Shakhtar regained the title in [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Vyshcha_Liha "2007–08 Vyshcha Liha"), also being victorious in the [Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Ukrainian_Cup "2007–08 Ukrainian Cup") after defeating Dynamo Kyiv 2–0 in the final.[[29]](#cite_note-30)
Shakhtar's attendance levels at league matches have continually risen over the years to a point where they averaged 36,983 spectators over the [2011–12 Premier League season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2011–12 Ukrainian Premier League").

### UEFA Cup triumph and domination in Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: UEFA Cup triumph and domination in Ukraine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Srna_vs_Anderlecht_%2818th_March_2016%29.jpg/250px-Srna_vs_Anderlecht_%2818th_March_2016%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Srna_vs_Anderlecht_(18th_March_2016).jpg)

Team captain [Darijo Srna](/wiki/Darijo_Srna "Darijo Srna"), one of the greatest players in the history of the team[[11]](#cite_note-sport.segodnya.ua-12) and considered by some "the [icon](/wiki/Pop_icon "Pop icon") of Shakhtar."[[30]](#cite_note-31)

In 2009, they became only the second Ukrainian team to win a European competition (and the first since independence), and the first to win the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/2009_UEFA_Cup_final "2009 UEFA Cup final") after defeating [Werder Bremen](/wiki/SV_Werder_Bremen "SV Werder Bremen") in the final, with goals from Brazilians [Luiz Adriano](/wiki/Luiz_Adriano "Luiz Adriano") and [Jádson](/wiki/J%C3%A1dson "Jádson").[[31]](#cite_note-32) The victory earned the player [Mariusz Lewandowski](/wiki/Mariusz_Lewandowski "Mariusz Lewandowski") the 2009 [Polish Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Pi%C5%82ka_no%C5%BCna_magazine_plebiscite "Piłka nożna magazine plebiscite") award. This also made them the last UEFA Cup winners before the tournament was rebranded as the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League").

Before the start of the 2009–10 season, Shakhtar won the friendly [Uhrencup](/wiki/Uhrencup "Uhrencup") tournament. Shakhtar won the Premier League title in the [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2009–10 Ukrainian Premier League"),[[32]](#cite_note-33) goalkeeper [Andriy Pyatov](/wiki/Andriy_Pyatov "Andriy Pyatov") was named Ukraine Premier League MVP by *Komanda*, and Manager Mircea Lucescu was named Romania Coach of the Year for the second time. The [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_season "2010–11 FC Shakhtar Donetsk season") was a very successful one for Shakhtar. They reached the quarter-finals of the Champions League, their then-best-ever performance in the competition.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Captain [Darijo Srna](/wiki/Darijo_Srna "Darijo Srna") was chosen to be part of the Champions League Team of the Season as voted by fans.[[34]](#cite_note-35) They also won a domestic treble with victory in the Premier League, [Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Ukrainian_Cup "2010–11 Ukrainian Cup") and the Super Cup.[[35]](#cite_note-36) The successful season did not go unnoticed by the experts, and in 2011, the [IFFHS](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Football_History_%26_Statistics "International Federation of Football History & Statistics") gave Shakhtar a special award for making the biggest progress of the decade among football clubs.[[36]](#cite_note-sports1-37)[[37]](#cite_note-shakhtar.com-38)

They went on to win the [Premier League](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2011–12 Ukrainian Premier League") and [Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Ukrainian_Cup "2011–12 Ukrainian Cup") in the [2011–12 season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_season "2011–12 FC Shakhtar Donetsk season").[[38]](#cite_note-39) Shakhtar player [Yevhen Seleznyov](/wiki/Yevhen_Seleznyov "Yevhen Seleznyov") topped the goal scoring charts in the league, with 14 goals, midfielder [Henrikh Mkhitaryan](/wiki/Henrikh_Mkhitaryan "Henrikh Mkhitaryan") was named [Armenian Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Armenian_Footballer_of_the_Year "Armenian Footballer of the Year"), and manager Mircea Lucescu was named 2012 Romania Coach of the Year, receiving the award for the third time. The main players at that time were captain Darijo Srna, defender [Yaroslav Rakitskyi](/wiki/Yaroslav_Rakitskyi "Yaroslav Rakitskyi"), Armenian midfielder Henrikh Mkhitaryan (who was named Armenian Footballer of the Year twice while playing for Shakhtar) and Brazilian midfielders [Fernandinho](/wiki/Fernandinho_(footballer,_born_May_1985) "Fernandinho (footballer, born May 1985)") and [Willian](/wiki/Willian_(footballer,_born_1988) "Willian (footballer, born 1988)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Heinrich_Mkhitaryan_2012.jpg/250px-Heinrich_Mkhitaryan_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heinrich_Mkhitaryan_2012.jpg)

[Henrikh Mkhitaryan](/wiki/Henrikh_Mkhitaryan "Henrikh Mkhitaryan") was named the 2012 [CIS Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Footballer_of_the_Year_in_Baltic_and_Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Footballer of the Year in Baltic and Commonwealth of Independent States")[[39]](#cite_note-40) and set the Ukrainian Premier League record for goals scored in one season (25).[[40]](#cite_note-41)

In the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2012–13 Ukrainian Premier League") Shakhtar won the [Premier League](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2012–13 Ukrainian Premier League"), [Cup](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Ukrainian_Cup "2012–13 Ukrainian Cup") and [Super Cup](/wiki/2013_Ukrainian_Cup_final "2013 Ukrainian Cup final"). Henrikh Mkhitaryan became the top scorer of the league, setting a Ukrainian championship record of 25 goals. He was also named the Ukraine Premier League MVP by *Komanda*, Armenian Footballer of the Year and the [CIS Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Footballer_of_the_Year_in_Baltic_and_Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Footballer of the Year in Baltic and Commonwealth of Independent States") for 2012.

### Leaders depart, new titles and war in Donbas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Leaders depart, new titles and war in Donbas")]

Prior to the 2013–14 season, many of the club's main players were sold after Shakhtar accepted high bids for them – Henrikh Mkhitaryan, Fernandinho and Willian brought the club over €100 million. Shakhtar spent the following summer trying to integrate new young players into the team, who along with the remaining players were to form the backbone of the renewed Shakhtar.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-sports.ru-44) Despite selling its leaders, before the 2013–14 season, Shakhtar set a new record for East Europe for number of season tickets sold.[[44]](#cite_note-ua-football.com-45) Before the beginning of the 2013–14 season, Shakhtar won two friendly tournaments in [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi "Abu Dhabi"), the Match World Cup, and the Super Cup of Champions played against Russian champions [Zenit Saint Petersburg](/wiki/FC_Zenit_Saint_Petersburg "FC Zenit Saint Petersburg").[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In the mid-season break, Shakhtar won the [2014 United Supercup](/wiki/2014_United_Supercup "2014 United Supercup") (the second edition of the [United Tournament](/wiki/United_Tournament "United Tournament")), a tournament between the top-two placed clubs of Ukraine and of Russia,[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48) which strengthened Shakhtar's status as the strongest club in Eastern Europe.[[43]](#cite_note-sports.ru-44)[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) At the end of the [2013–14](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2013–14 Ukrainian Premier League") season, Shakhtar won the Ukraine Premier League, while [Luiz Adriano](/wiki/Luiz_Adriano "Luiz Adriano") was the league top scorer. Shakhtar also won the 2014 Ukrainian Super Cup, holding the trophy for the sixth time. Manager Mircea Lucescu was named the 2014 Romania Coach of the Year, receiving the award for the fourth time.

Due to the war in Donbas, Shakhtar had to temporarily move and play its games in [Arena Lviv](/wiki/Arena_Lviv "Arena Lviv"),[[50]](#cite_note-51) resulting in very low attendance.[[51]](#cite_note-52) As an anti-war protest, the players of Shakhtar refused the initiative to wear the "Glory to the Ukrainian Army" shirts.[[52]](#cite_note-53) In the [2014–15 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_UEFA_Champions_League "2014–15 UEFA Champions League"), Shakhtar finished second in the group stage, therefore qualifying to the next stage. Striker Luiz Adriano equaled both [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi")'s record of five goals in a Champions League match and [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo")'s record of scoring nine goals in the group stage; as a result, [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") named him MVP of the competition's group stage.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Shakhtar finished the season second in the [2014–15 Ukrainian Premier League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2014–15 Ukrainian Premier League") after playing the whole season away from Donbas, with [Alex Teixeira](/wiki/Alex_Teixeira "Alex Teixeira") finishing as a joint top scorer in the league. At the end of the season, [Douglas Costa](/wiki/Douglas_Costa "Douglas Costa") was sold to [Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich"),[[54]](#cite_note-55) while Luiz Adriano moved to [Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan").[[55]](#cite_note-56)

While the club itself moved to a [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")-controlled zone, a few prominent Shakhtar players remained in the [Donetsk People's Republic](/wiki/Donetsk_People%27s_Republic "Donetsk People's Republic") and supported the unrecognised state. Among them were former defender [Viktor Zvyahintsev](/wiki/Viktor_Zvyahintsev "Viktor Zvyahintsev"), former goalkeeper [Yuriy Dehteryov](/wiki/Yuriy_Dehteryov "Yuriy Dehteryov"), former Shakhtar and [Ukraine national football team](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team") captain [Ihor Petrov](/wiki/Ihor_Petrov "Ihor Petrov"), and the club's first press officer Vyacheslav Sharafutdinov.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)

During the [2015–16 Ukrainian Premier League](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Ukrainian_Premier_League "2015–16 Ukrainian Premier League"), on 16 October, Shakhtar beat Dynamo Kyiv 3–0 in Kyiv and set two new records. One record was that for the first time during a [Klasychne derby](/wiki/Klasychne_derby "Klasychne derby") game in Kyiv a team scored three goals. The other record was that for the first time Shakhtar had more [Klasychne derby](/wiki/Klasychne_derby "Klasychne derby") victories, 26, than Dynamo.[[58]](#cite_note-59) In the middle of the season, Alex Teixeira moved to Chinese club [Jiangsu Suning](/wiki/Jiangsu_F.C. "Jiangsu F.C.") for a fee of €50 million,[[59]](#cite_note-60) breaking both the Asian and Ukrainian[[60]](#cite_note-61) transfer record.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) The club finished the 2015/16 season as runner up and [Marlos](/wiki/Marlos "Marlos") was recognised best league player by Komanda. After the 2015–16 season, long-time manager Mircea Lucescu moved on to Zenit Saint Petersburg; he was replaced by the Portuguese [Paulo Fonseca](/wiki/Paulo_Fonseca "Paulo Fonseca"), previously of [Braga](/wiki/S.C._Braga "S.C. Braga").

Following the winter break of the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_season "2016–17 FC Shakhtar Donetsk season"), a season when the club won the league, the cup, and the supercup, the club moved to the [Metalist Stadium](/wiki/Metalist_Oblast_Sports_Complex "Metalist Oblast Sports Complex") in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") (241 km to the north of Donetsk).[[1]](#cite_note-ShaktarKharkiv-1) In 2017–18, the club won the league and the cup again, with [Facundo Ferreyra](/wiki/Facundo_Ferreyra "Facundo Ferreyra") becoming the league top scorer and [Marlos](/wiki/Marlos "Marlos") the league assist leader. In the 2018–19 season, the club won Ukrainian Premier League for the third time in a row.[[63]](#cite_note-64) In 2019–20, Shakhtar retained their league title for the fourth time in a row. They also reached the semi-finals of the [2019–20 UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Europa_League "2019–20 UEFA Europa League").[[64]](#cite_note-65)

[Shakhtar Stadium](/wiki/Shakhtar_Stadium_(Donetsk) "Shakhtar Stadium (Donetsk)") as seen from [tailings](/wiki/Tailings "Tailings") (locally terra-cone)

Until 2009, Shakhtar had been playing most of its games at the [RSC Olimpiyskyi](/wiki/RSC_Olimpiyskiy "RSC Olimpiyskiy") stadium, which is a property of the administration of [Donetsk Oblast](/wiki/Donetsk_Oblast "Donetsk Oblast") and the [Serhiy Bubka College of Olympic Reserve](/wiki/Serhiy_Bubka_College_of_Olympic_Reserve "Serhiy Bubka College of Olympic Reserve"). The stadium was built during the Soviet period for another Donetsk club, [FC Lokomotyv Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Lokomotyv_Donetsk "FC Lokomotyv Donetsk"), and carried its name.

On 29 August 2009, the construction of its new stadium, [Donbas Arena](/wiki/Donbass_Arena "Donbass Arena"), was accomplished, and the stadium was opened. Located in the [City Park of Culture and Recreation](/wiki/Donetsk_Culture_and_Leasure_Park "Donetsk Culture and Leasure Park"), it has a capacity of 50,149 and has been honored with a [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") five-star rating, the highest rating achievable.

Shakhtar's old home, the [Central Stadium "Shakhtar"](/wiki/Shakhtar_Stadium_(Donetsk) "Shakhtar Stadium (Donetsk)"), which was built in 1936 and reconstructed four times, had been used by [Shakhtar Donetsk Reserves](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_junior_squads_and_academy "FC Shakhtar Donetsk junior squads and academy"). The stadium received some major renovations, including the installation of bench seats in 2000 when Shakhtar made it to the [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") Group Stage.

A [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot") mole (*moles* is a nickname for the club) will entertain spectators during the home matches. Shakhtar are rated 40th by the average game attendance, being the top Eastern European club on the rating charts.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Before the 2013–14 season, Shakhtar set a new record for [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") for number of season tickets sold, selling 27,000 season tickets, which means 52% of the seats in Donbas Arena belong to season ticket holders.[[44]](#cite_note-ua-football.com-45)

From 2014 until the end of 2016, due to the [war in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas_(2014%E2%80%932022) "War in Donbas (2014–2022)"), Shakhtar played its home matches at the [Arena Lviv](/wiki/Arena_Lviv "Arena Lviv").[[66]](#cite_note-PFL_schedule-67)[[67]](#cite_note-Shakhtar_to_play_home_matches_in_Lviv-68) Following the winter break of the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_season "2016–17 FC Shakhtar Donetsk season") the club moved to the [Metalist Stadium](/wiki/Metalist_Oblast_Sports_Complex "Metalist Oblast Sports Complex") in [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") (250 kilometers to the northwest of Donetsk).[[1]](#cite_note-ShaktarKharkiv-1) Shakhtar played their 2022–23 matches in the [Champions League](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Champions_League "2022–23 UEFA Champions League") and [Europa League](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Europa_League "2022–23 UEFA Europa League") at [Stadion Wojska Polskiego](/wiki/Stadion_Wojska_Polskiego "Stadion Wojska Polskiego") in [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") due to the 2022 invasion. They played their [2023–24 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_UEFA_Champions_League "2023–24 UEFA Champions League") and [2023–24 UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_UEFA_Europa_League "2023–24 UEFA Europa League") matches at [Volksparkstadion](/wiki/Volksparkstadion "Volksparkstadion") in [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), Germany.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Next season of their [2024–25 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Champions_League "2024–25 UEFA Champions League") Shakhtar played at [Arena AufSchalke](/wiki/Arena_AufSchalke "Arena AufSchalke") in [Gelsenkirchen](/wiki/Gelsenkirchen "Gelsenkirchen"), Germany. Their [2025–26 UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_UEFA_Europa_League "2025–26 UEFA Europa League") Shakhtar started out at [Stožice Stadium](/wiki/Sto%C5%BEice_Stadium "Stožice Stadium") in [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"), Slovenia and later [Henryk Reyman Municipal Stadium](/wiki/Henryk_Reyman_Municipal_Stadium "Henryk Reyman Municipal Stadium") in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), Poland.

### Training centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Training centre")]

Shakhtar Donetsk has its training facilities for its first team, reserve team, and youth academy, all located at [Kirsha Training Centre](/wiki/Kirsha_Training_Centre "Kirsha Training Centre").

Due to the war conditions in the [eastern Ukraine](/wiki/Eastern_Ukraine "Eastern Ukraine"), Shakhtar temporary venue for its home matches has changed several times, while it was announced that the team will use training facilities in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv").[[67]](#cite_note-Shakhtar_to_play_home_matches_in_Lviv-68) It was clarified that its training facility is the Training base of Olympic Preparation "[Sviatoshyn](/wiki/Sviatoshyn "Sviatoshyn")" located 20 km (12 mi) away from Kyiv and belongs to the Ukrainian Federation of Trade Unions.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)

### Youth, academy and reserves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Youth, academy and reserves")]

The club used to field a couple of reserve teams that competed at a professional level. By 2015, all reserve teams, such as [FC Shakhtar-2 Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar-2_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar-2 Donetsk") and [FC Shakhtar-3 Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar-3_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar-3 Donetsk") were withdrawn from professional competitions. The club, however, fields its youth [Shakhtar U-21](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_junior_squads_and_academy "FC Shakhtar Donetsk junior squads and academy") team in the youth championship of [Ukrainian Premier League](/wiki/Ukrainian_Premier_League "Ukrainian Premier League"). Shakhtar also has its football academy that fields four teams in a special youth league designated for teenagers. Since 2012, the club has also had a team for the U-19 championship of the Ukrainian Premier League.

During the [Soviet times](/wiki/Football_in_the_Soviet_Union "Football in the Soviet Union"), the club used to have one youth team named Shakhter-D Donetsk that participated in a separate Soviet championship for doubles. Shakhter-D later was reorganized into [FC Shakhtar-2 Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar-2_Donetsk "FC Shakhtar-2 Donetsk") and admitted to the [Ukrainian First League](/wiki/Ukrainian_First_League "Ukrainian First League").

Due to the [2022 invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War#2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russo-Ukrainian War"), the youth academy has relocated to [Split, Croatia](/wiki/Split,_Croatia "Split, Croatia").[[72]](#cite_note-73)

## Crests and colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Crests and colours")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Shakhtar_Donetsk_Advertisement.JPG/250px-Shakhtar_Donetsk_Advertisement.JPG)](/wiki/File:Shakhtar_Donetsk_Advertisement.JPG)

A public billboard in Donetsk in 2009, from the local "[Konti](/wiki/Konti_Group "Konti Group")" confectionary with compliments for the club

The first logo of the club was designed in 1936; it featured a blue [hexagon](/wiki/Hexagon "Hexagon"), with a red 'S' in the middle, crossed over by a [jackhammer](/wiki/Jackhammer "Jackhammer"). In 1946, when the club was renamed, the logo was changed to black and white, with the addition of the club's name. Later, in the middle of the 1960s, their logo depicted two crossed hammers, with "Shakhtar Donetsk" written in the circle. The crest was added to the kit and remained there, except for several seasons at the beginning of the 1990s. The club's name was depicted in the Russian language until the latest logo was chosen. Therefore, some sources have their name written often as "Shakhter" or rarely "Shakhtyor."

In 1989, an artist, Viktor Savilov, on the occasion of the club restructuring, offered a draft variant of a logo with elements of the [ball](/wiki/Ball "Ball") and a [pitch](/wiki/Association_football_pitch "Association football pitch"). Sometime later, the logo was remodelled into the present one. The emblem was added to the kit in 1997.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

In 2007, during the presentation of the club's new stadium, Shakhtar's new logo was unveiled. For the first time in over 30 years, the crossed hammers, the traditional symbols of the club, were present on the crest. Also, for the first time, the name was written in the Ukrainian language and not Russian.

Since 1961, the official colours have been black and orange.

## Supporters and rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Supporters and rivalries")]

See also: [Klasychne derby](/wiki/Klasychne_derby "Klasychne derby") and [Donetsk derby](/wiki/Donetsk_derby "Donetsk derby")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Ultras_in_the_Donbas_Arena%2C_2009.JPG/250px-Ultras_in_the_Donbas_Arena%2C_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ultras_in_the_Donbas_Arena,_2009.JPG)

Shakhtar ultras at the [Donbas Arena](/wiki/Donbass_Arena "Donbass Arena")

The first riots associated with Shakhtar fans took place on 20 September 1959 in the match against [CSKA Moscow](/wiki/PFC_CSKA_Moscow "PFC CSKA Moscow"), when several dozen Shakhtar supporters ran onto the field, and as a result the match was frustrated. In all matches involving Donetsk, many people came that promoted the development of fan movement in Donetsk. Active development of football movement began in the early 1980s. In the early 2000s to the Ukrainian stadiums came [English style](/wiki/Football_hooliganism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Football hooliganism in the United Kingdom") – [hooliganism](/wiki/Football_hooliganism "Football hooliganism"). In 2003, during the final of the [Ukrainian Cup](/wiki/Ukrainian_Cup "Ukrainian Cup") there was a fierce fight between [Dynamo](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv") and Shakhtar fans.

The other rivalry was with [Metalurh Donetsk](/wiki/FC_Metalurh_Donetsk "FC Metalurh Donetsk"). This was another local club and, although not as significant as games against the rivals from the capital, the games between the two Donetsk teams were proclaimed the [Donetsk derby](/wiki/Donetsk_derby "Donetsk derby"). Metalurh went bankrupt in July 2015.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

Among the extinguished rivalries are the games against [Spartak Moscow](/wiki/FC_Spartak_Moscow "FC Spartak Moscow") and, particularly, the *third place champions* [Dinamo Tbilisi](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Tbilisi "FC Dinamo Tbilisi") of Georgia that took place at times during the [Soviet Top League](/wiki/Soviet_Top_League "Soviet Top League"). Another interesting rivalry, the Donbas Derby, is with [Zorya Luhansk](/wiki/FC_Zorya_Luhansk "FC Zorya Luhansk"), which gathered a significant crowd in [Luhansk](/wiki/Luhansk "Luhansk"). During the early Ukrainian championships, another interesting rivalry developed with [Chornomorets Odesa](/wiki/FC_Chornomorets_Odesa "FC Chornomorets Odesa") labelled the "Miners vs. Sailors", which declined with the turn of the millennium due to the inconsistent performance of the [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa")-based club.

## European history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: European history")]

Main article: [FC Shakhtar Donetsk in European football](/wiki/FC_Shakhtar_Donetsk_in_European_football "FC Shakhtar Donetsk in European football")

Shakhtar Donetsk has participated in European competition since 1976, playing its first game against [Berliner FC Dynamo](/wiki/Berliner_FC_Dynamo "Berliner FC Dynamo") in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_UEFA_Cup "1976–77 UEFA Cup"). Since 1997, the club has participated in UEFA competition annually with variable amounts of success, and first took part in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") competition in 2000. Shakhtar Donetsk played against [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), [Lazio](/wiki/SS_Lazio "SS Lazio") and [Sparta Prague](/wiki/AC_Sparta_Prague "AC Sparta Prague") upon qualifying for the group stage for the first time in [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_UEFA_Champions_League "2000–01 UEFA Champions League").
